region_of_origin: India (Punjab / North India); widely adopted in South Asia and globally

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Murgh Tikka Masala; A0CKW (chicken curry dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Murgh Tikka Masala or chicken tikka masala as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Murgh Tikka Masala is approximately 350 g, comprising tandoor-roasted chicken pieces in a rich tomato-cream masala sauce, usually accompanied by naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken tikka masala, restaurant-style — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Chicken tikka masala (NDB 35193)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides values for Indian chicken curry dishes. USDA SR Legacy entry for chicken tikka masala (FDC ID 174825) used as primary numeric anchor. Values represent a typical restaurant-style preparation with cream and tomato-based sauce. Per-100g values are derived from the USDA SR Legacy record and cross-checked against ICMR-NIN Indian chicken curry data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed Indian curry dishes — analogous estimate based on chicken curry with tomato-cream sauce; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Murgh Tikka Masala found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed Indian chicken curry dishes with tomato-cream sauce and moderate carbohydrate content. The dish is protein- and fat-rich with a tomato-cream base; fat and protein slow gastric emptying, supporting a low GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 23.8) / 100 ≈ 10.7, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 6.8 × 3.5 = 23.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-salt preparation, as a single restaurant serving can contribute approximately 60–70% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~10.7 per serving) and cream-based sauce contributing saturated fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Murgh Tikka Masala has a low estimated glycemic index (~45) and moderate glycemic load per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, pair with a low-GI side such as basmati rice or whole-wheat roti, and be mindful of the cream content which adds saturated fat and total calories.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream and ghee (~3.2 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The cream and ghee in Murgh Tikka Masala contribute saturated fat that may raise LDL cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose smaller portions, request reduced cream preparation, and balance the meal with high-fibre accompaniments to mitigate cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murgh Tikka Masala (also widely known as Chicken Tikka Masala) is a classic North Indian curry dish in which boneless chicken pieces are first marinated in spiced yoghurt and roasted in a tandoor (clay oven) until lightly charred, then simmered in a rich, aromatic sauce made from tomatoes, cream, onions, garlic, ginger, and a blend of spices including garam masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves). The result is a dish with tender, smoky chicken pieces enveloped in a velvety, mildly spiced, slightly tangy and creamy orange-red sauce. The flavour profile is complex: warm spice notes from garam masala, gentle heat from chilli, sweetness from tomato and cream, and a distinctive smokiness from the tandoor-roasted chicken. Murgh Tikka Masala is one of the most popular Indian restaurant dishes globally and is a staple on menus across Singapore, the United Kingdom, and the Indian diaspora worldwide. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-calorie, protein-rich dish (~12.5 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat (~8.5 g per 100 g), primarily from cream and ghee. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index (~45) due to its high protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying. Clinically, the main concerns are sodium content (relevant for hypertension), saturated fat from cream (relevant for cardiovascular disease), and total caloric density when consumed in large restaurant portions. It is typically served with naan, roti, or basmati rice. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified chicken and no alcohol-based marinades.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken tikka masala?
Is murgh tikka masala high in sodium?
Can diabetics eat chicken tikka masala?
What is the glycemic index of chicken tikka masala?
What spices are in murgh tikka masala?
Difference between murgh tikka masala and butter chicken
meal_context: paired with naan or basmati rice

ingredient_synonyms.garam masala: warm spice blend (cumin, coriander, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, black pepper)
ingredient_synonyms.tandoor: traditional clay oven used for high-heat roasting

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 174825 (chicken tikka masala) cross-checked against ICMR-NIN Indian chicken curry data. The USDA entry is a reasonable match for restaurant-style Murgh Tikka Masala but recipe variation across restaurants means exact values will differ. GI is an analogous estimate with no direct measurement available. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source record for this dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source record for this dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source record for this dish. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the primary source. GI is an analogous estimate at 45 (low) based on comparable mixed Indian chicken curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as (45 × 23.8) / 100 ≈ 10.7 g. The dish is served at Kinara - Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 19.00. Recipe composition and nutrient values may vary by restaurant. The description in the input record contained a typographical error ('Clay ovedned' corrected to 'clay oven-roasted / tandoor-roasted'). FoodOn URI FOODON_03302757 is used as the best available exact match for chicken tikka masala. FoodEx2 code A0CKW is used as closest parent (chicken curry dish) as no specific Murgh Tikka Masala code exists in FoodEx2.

Estimated GI at 45 (low) from analogous Indian chicken curry data; documented estimation basis in gi_source and notes.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
